news_summary: Scientists have made breakthrough discoveries unveiling new insights into cosmic phenomena, including a mysterious asteroid disintegrating near the Sun, the century-old mystery behind cosmic rays, and an ancient powerful solar storm recorded in tree rings.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont fait des découvertes majeures qui apportent un nouvel éclairage sur certains phénomènes cosmiques, notamment la désintégration d'un mystérieux astéroïde près du Soleil, le mystère vieux d'un siècle entourant les rayons cosmiques, ainsi qu'une puissante tempête solaire ancienne dont les traces ont été conservées dans les cernes des arbres.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han realizado descubrimientos revolucionarios que arrojan nueva luz sobre fenómenos cósmicos, entre ellos la desintegración de un misterioso asteroide cerca del Sol, el misterio centenario que se esconde tras los rayos cósmicos y una antigua y potente tormenta solar registrada en los anillos de los árboles.

articles.title: Scientists discover a mysterious asteroid breaking apart near the Sun
articles.summary: A newly discovered meteor stream may be the smoking gun of an asteroid slowly disintegrating under the Sun’s intense heat. Scientists say these fiery streaks across the night sky could reveal hidden near-Earth asteroids that telescopes struggle to detect.
articles.content:
Every night around the world, thousands of automated sky cameras watch for flashes of light streaking across the atmosphere. I am one of the scientists who studies these meteors and what they can reveal about our solar system.

Popular movies and breaking news alerts usually focus on giant asteroids that could threaten Earth. Every few months, people also hear about another large space rock making a close flyby. But the much smaller particles that constantly enter Earth's atmosphere every day can tell scientists an equally fascinating story.

My colleagues in planetary science and I analyze images from night sky camera networks to study cosmic dust, small asteroids and debris left behind by comets.

In research published in March 2026, I examined millions of meteor detections recorded by all sky camera systems in Canada, Japan, California and Europe. During that search, I identified a small cluster of recently formed meteors. The 282 meteors in this group appear to trace back to an asteroid that ventured dangerously close to the Sun.

How Meteors Form

When a tiny grain of rock from space enters Earth's atmosphere, it rapidly heats up. Its outer layer vaporizes and becomes an electrically charged gas, causing the object to glow brightly. That glowing streak is what scientists call a meteor. Larger incoming objects, such as boulder sized rocks that create especially bright flashes, are known as bolides or fireballs.

These objects usually slam into the atmosphere at speeds exceeding 15 miles per second. For very small particles, the entire event lasts only a split second before the material completely burns up.

Most tiny fragments that create meteors originally come from comets, icy objects that formed in the cold outer solar system. As a comet approaches the Sun, its ice turns directly into gas and releases huge amounts of dust into space. This process gives comets their fuzzy appearance and explains why astronomers often refer to them as "dirty snowballs."

Asteroids are different. They formed closer to the Sun early in the solar system's history and are generally dry, rocky bodies without the icy material that produces comet tails.

What Makes an Asteroid "Active"?

Astronomers describe an asteroid or comet as "active" when it releases dust, gas or larger pieces of material into space. Several different forces can trigger this activity, including heat from the Sun, collisions or extremely rapid spinning that causes the object to break apart.

Studying this activity helps scientists understand how asteroids and comets physically evolve over time.

For comets, the main driver is usually sublimation of ice. This happens when solid ice changes directly into gas without first becoming liquid. Asteroids, however, can become active for a variety of reasons.

NASA's OSIRIS-REx mission, which visited the asteroid Bennu, observed material erupting from the asteroid's surface. Scientists believe heat stress and tiny impacts were among the most likely causes.

Other possible explanations for asteroid activity include rotational breakup, gravitational tidal forces during close planetary encounters and the release of trapped gases.

Astronomers often look for signs of activity using telescopes. A visible tail or a fuzzy cloud surrounding an object usually signals the presence of gas and dust. But meteor showers provide another powerful way to detect active objects in space.

Meteor Showers Can Reveal Hidden Asteroids

One of the best known active asteroids is 3200 Phaethon, the source of the Geminid meteor shower that peaks every December. During close passes near the Sun, Phaethon released large amounts of dust and debris. Over time, those fragments spread out along the asteroid's orbit, creating the meteor stream Earth passes through each year.

Meteor showers occur whenever Earth moves through one of these streams of debris. Because of this, meteor showers can help astronomers uncover active asteroids and comets that might otherwise remain hidden.

When debris first separates from an asteroid or comet, the fragments stay tightly grouped together. Imagine squeezing a drop of food coloring into flowing water. At first, the color remains concentrated, but the movement of the water gradually spreads it apart.

A similar process happens in space. Gravitational pulls from planets slowly tug on the individual fragments in different ways, causing the once compact stream to spread out and eventually blend into the background dust filling the solar system.

Discovery of a Sun-Baked "Rock-Comet"

In my March 2026 study published in the Astrophysical Journal, I searched through millions of meteor observations looking for evidence of previously unknown asteroid activity near Earth. One cluster containing 282 meteors clearly stood out.

The discovery is exciting because it appears to capture an asteroid actively breaking down under intense solar heat. The newly identified meteor stream follows an extreme orbit that carries it nearly five times closer to the Sun than Earth's orbit.

By studying how these meteors fragment in Earth's atmosphere, scientists can tell they are somewhat fragile, although still stronger than typical comet material. The evidence suggests that extreme heating from the Sun is cracking the asteroid's surface, releasing trapped gases and causing the body to slowly crumble apart.

This same process may explain much of the past activity seen from Phaethon and could also help explain why meteorites found on Earth vary so widely in composition and structure.

Searching for the Parent Asteroid

Finding a hidden asteroid that is actively falling apart is important because meteor observations can reveal objects that ordinary telescopes cannot detect.

In addition to solving scientific mysteries, studying this debris helps researchers better understand how asteroids and comets change over time. It also uncovers previously hidden populations of near Earth asteroids, which is valuable information for planetary defense efforts.

Scientists still have not identified the parent asteroid responsible for this newly discovered meteor shower. However, NASA's NEO Surveyor mission, scheduled to launch in 2027, could help solve the mystery.

The spacecraft is specifically designed to detect dark, potentially hazardous asteroids that travel close to the Sun. That makes it an ideal tool for tracking down the source of this unusual meteor stream.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of an asteroid actively breaking apart near the Sun, revealed through meteor observations. This finding advances understanding of asteroid behavior, helps uncover hidden near-Earth objects, and supports planetary defense efforts, thus having broad scientific and societal impact. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, its implications, and future missions to further investigate, meeting criteria for inspiring and substantial good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find asteroid breaking apart near the Sun using meteor clues
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study meteors, which are bright streaks caused by space dust and rocks burning up in Earth’s atmosphere. These small pieces often come from comets or asteroids and can tell us about objects in space that are hard to see with telescopes.

**What happened** – In March 2026, researchers analyzed millions of meteor records from cameras in Canada, Japan, California, and Europe. They found a group of 282 meteors that seem to come from an asteroid passing very close to the Sun. This asteroid is slowly breaking apart because of the Sun’s intense heat, releasing dust and gas.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows how some asteroids can become “active” and crumble when heated by the Sun. It also helps scientists find asteroids that are usually hidden from telescopes. Understanding these asteroids improves our knowledge of how space rocks change over time and helps protect Earth from possible asteroid threats.

**What’s next step** – Scientists have not yet found the exact asteroid causing this meteor stream. NASA’s NEO Surveyor mission, launching in 2027, will look for dark and hidden asteroids near the Sun and might identify the source of these meteors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered a new meteor stream revealing an asteroid breaking apart near the Sun, offering fresh insights into asteroid behavior and helping track hidden space rocks near Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un astéroïde se désintégrant près du Soleil grâce aux météores
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les météores, ces traînées lumineuses causées par des poussières et des roches spatiales qui brûlent dans l’atmosphère terrestre. Ces petits morceaux viennent souvent de comètes ou d’astéroïdes et peuvent révéler des objets dans l’espace difficiles à voir avec les télescopes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, des chercheurs ont analysé des millions d’observations de météores provenant de caméras au Canada, au Japon, en Californie et en Europe. Ils ont trouvé un groupe de 282 météores provenant d’un astéroïde s’approchant très près du Soleil. Cet astéroïde se désintègre lentement à cause de la chaleur intense du Soleil, libérant poussières et gaz.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre comment certains astéroïdes peuvent devenir “actifs” et se briser lorsqu’ils sont chauffés par le Soleil. Elle aide aussi les scientifiques à détecter des astéroïdes cachés aux télescopes. Comprendre ces astéroïdes améliore notre connaissance de l’évolution des roches spatiales et contribue à protéger la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques n’ont pas encore identifié l’astéroïde responsable de ce courant de météores. La mission NEO Surveyor de la NASA, qui sera lancée en 2027, cherchera les astéroïdes sombres et cachés près du Soleil et pourrait trouver la source de ces météores.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert un nouveau courant de météores révélant un astéroïde se désintégrant près du Soleil, offrant de nouvelles informations sur le comportement des astéroïdes et aidant à repérer des roches spatiales cachées près de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren asteroide desintegrándose cerca del Sol gracias a meteoros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian los meteoros, que son rayas brillantes causadas por polvo y rocas espaciales que se queman en la atmósfera de la Tierra. Estos pequeños fragmentos suelen venir de cometas o asteroides y pueden revelar objetos en el espacio difíciles de ver con telescopios.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026, investigadores analizaron millones de registros de meteoros de cámaras en Canadá, Japón, California y Europa. Encontraron un grupo de 282 meteoros que parecen venir de un asteroide que pasa muy cerca del Sol. Este asteroide se está desintegrando lentamente por el intenso calor solar, liberando polvo y gas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra cómo algunos asteroides pueden volverse “activos” y desmoronarse al calentarse por el Sol. También ayuda a los científicos a encontrar asteroides que normalmente están ocultos para los telescopios. Entender estos asteroides mejora nuestro conocimiento sobre cómo cambian las rocas espaciales y ayuda a proteger la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos aún no han encontrado el asteroide exacto que causa esta lluvia de meteoros. La misión NEO Surveyor de la NASA, que se lanzará en 2027, buscará asteroides oscuros y ocultos cerca del Sol y podría identificar la fuente de estos meteoros.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron una nueva lluvia de meteoros que revela un asteroide desintegrándose cerca del Sol, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre el comportamiento de los asteroides y ayudando a rastrear rocas espaciales ocultas cerca de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a glowing, fragmented asteroid orbiting close to a radiant, warm sun, with a gentle stream of bright meteor streaks trailing behind it as they enter Earth’s atmosphere, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and warm yellows, capturing the asteroid’s slow breakup and the celestial dance of dust and debris in space.

articles.title: After 100 years, scientists finally uncover hidden rule behind cosmic rays
articles.summary: Scientists studying mysterious ultra-powerful cosmic rays have uncovered a surprising hidden pattern that could finally help explain where these particles come from. Using the DAMPE space telescope, researchers found that cosmic ray particles—from tiny protons to heavy iron nuclei—all begin fading away more sharply at the exact same point, hinting at a universal rule governing their behavior across the galaxy.
articles.content:
For more than 100 years, scientists have been trying to understand cosmic rays, incredibly powerful particles that travel across the universe at extreme energies. Despite decades of research, many questions about where they come from and how they are accelerated remain unanswered. Now, researchers working with the DAMPE (Dark Matter Particle Explorer) space telescope have uncovered an important new clue. Their findings, published in Nature, reveal a common feature shared by these mysterious particles and could help scientists better understand their origins.

Cosmic rays are the highest energy particles ever observed in nature. They carry far more energy than particles produced by even the most advanced accelerators on Earth. Scientists believe they are created by some of the universe's most violent events, including supernova explosions, jets from black holes, and pulsars.

Launched in December 2015, the DAMPE space telescope was designed to investigate the nature of cosmic rays and explore possible connections to dark matter. The mission includes major contributions from the astrophysics group at the Department of Nuclear and Particle Physics (DPNC) at the University of Geneva (UNIGE).

By examining highly precise data collected by DAMPE, researchers discovered a universal pattern in the energy spectra of primary cosmic ray nuclei, ranging from lightweight protons to much heavier iron nuclei.

"Cosmic rays are primarily composed of protons, but also of helium, carbon, oxygen, and iron nuclei,'' explains Andrii Tykhonov, associate professor at the DPNC in the Faculty of Science at UNIGE, and co-author of the study. "These particles are also categorised according to their energy: low, up to a few billion electron-volts; intermediate, from a few billion to several hundred billion electron-volts; and high, from 1,000 billion electron-volts and beyond."

Scientists Discover a Shared Cosmic Ray Pattern

The research showed that for every type of nucleus studied, the number of particles begins dropping much faster after reaching a certain threshold. Scientists refer to this effect as "spectral softening."

Normally, higher-energy cosmic rays become less common as energy increases. However, the DAMPE observations revealed that the decline becomes dramatically steeper beyond a rigidity of roughly 15 TV (teraelectron-volts). Rigidity describes how strongly a particle's path resists being bent by magnetic fields.

Because this same feature appears across many different types of particles, the findings strongly support theories suggesting that cosmic ray acceleration and movement through space are controlled by rigidity. At the same time, the data largely rules out competing explanations based on energy per nucleon (energy divided by the number of nucleons in the particle). According to the researchers, the confidence level against those alternative models reaches 99.999%.

AI and Advanced Detectors Help Drive the Discovery

Researchers from Geneva played a major role in the breakthrough. The team developed sophisticated artificial intelligence methods to reconstruct particle events detected by the telescope. They also contributed to important measurements involving proton and helium fluxes and helped analyze carbon nuclei data.

In addition, the Geneva group led the development of one of DAMPE's key instruments, the Silicon-Tungsten Tracker (STK). This detector is essential for accurately tracing particle paths and determining the electrical charge of incoming cosmic rays.

The findings mark an important advance in understanding how cosmic rays are created and how they travel through the galaxy. Scientists say the new results place tighter limits on existing models of particle acceleration in astrophysical sources and improve our understanding of how high-energy particles move through interstellar space.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding cosmic rays, a century-old mystery in astrophysics. This discovery, enabled by advanced space telescope data and AI methods, has broad implications for our knowledge of the universe and particle acceleration processes, benefiting the scientific community and humanity's understanding of space.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover universal pattern in cosmic rays after 100 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over a century, scientists have studied cosmic rays, which are extremely powerful particles traveling through space. Despite many efforts, their exact origins and how they gain such high energy remained unclear.

**What happened** – Researchers using the DAMPE space telescope found a surprising pattern in cosmic rays. They noticed that particles like protons and iron nuclei all start fading away sharply at the same energy level, a point called 15 teraelectron-volts (TV). This shows a common rule affecting how these particles behave in space.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it suggests that cosmic rays are controlled by a property called rigidity, which relates to how particles move through magnetic fields. It helps scientists better understand how cosmic rays are created and travel, ruling out other ideas about their behavior with high confidence.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this new pattern to improve models of cosmic ray acceleration and movement. This could lead to clearer answers about the violent events in space, like supernova explosions or black holes, that produce these particles.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using the DAMPE telescope, scientists found a shared energy pattern in cosmic rays, revealing a universal rule that helps explain their mysterious origins and journey through space.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un motif universel dans les rayons cosmiques après 100 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus d’un siècle, les scientifiques étudient les rayons cosmiques, des particules très puissantes voyageant dans l’espace. Malgré de nombreuses recherches, leurs origines exactes et la façon dont elles acquièrent une si grande énergie restaient inconnues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs utilisant le télescope spatial DAMPE ont découvert un motif surprenant dans les rayons cosmiques. Ils ont remarqué que des particules comme les protons et les noyaux de fer commencent toutes à diminuer rapidement au même niveau d’énergie, appelé 15 téraélectronvolts (TV). Cela montre une règle commune qui influence leur comportement dans l’espace.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle suggère que les rayons cosmiques sont contrôlés par une propriété appelée rigidité, liée à la façon dont les particules se déplacent dans les champs magnétiques. Elle aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment les rayons cosmiques sont créés et voyagent, en écartant d'autres idées avec une grande certitude.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront ce nouveau motif pour améliorer les modèles d’accélération et de déplacement des rayons cosmiques. Cela pourrait permettre de mieux comprendre les événements violents dans l’espace, comme les explosions de supernova ou les trous noirs, qui produisent ces particules.

**Phrase clé** – Grâce au télescope DAMPE, les scientifiques ont trouvé un motif énergétique commun dans les rayons cosmiques, révélant une règle universelle qui aide à expliquer leurs origines mystérieuses et leur voyage dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un patrón universal en los rayos cósmicos tras 100 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de un siglo, los científicos han estudiado los rayos cósmicos, partículas muy poderosas que viajan por el espacio. A pesar de muchos esfuerzos, sus orígenes exactos y cómo adquieren tanta energía seguían sin estar claros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores que usaron el telescopio espacial DAMPE encontraron un patrón sorprendente en los rayos cósmicos. Notaron que partículas como protones y núcleos de hierro comienzan a disminuir rápidamente en el mismo nivel de energía, llamado 15 teraelectronvoltios (TV). Esto muestra una regla común que afecta cómo se comportan estas partículas en el espacio.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque sugiere que los rayos cósmicos están controlados por una propiedad llamada rigidez, que tiene que ver con cómo las partículas se mueven a través de campos magnéticos. Ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se crean y viajan los rayos cósmicos, descartando otras ideas con alta confianza.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este nuevo patrón para mejorar los modelos de aceleración y movimiento de los rayos cósmicos. Esto podría llevar a respuestas más claras sobre los eventos violentos en el espacio, como explosiones de supernovas o agujeros negros, que producen estas partículas.

**Frase clave** – Usando el telescopio DAMPE, los científicos encontraron un patrón energético común en los rayos cósmicos, revelando una regla universal que ayuda a explicar sus orígenes misteriosos y su viaje por el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a cosmic landscape featuring a stylized space telescope orbiting a glowing, swirling galaxy, with colorful streams of energetic particles—depicted as vibrant, flowing ribbons of light in varying sizes and hues—radiating outward and converging on a central point symbolizing the universal pattern discovered in cosmic ray energies.

articles.title: Deadly “red sky” solar storm from 800 years ago discovered in ancient trees
articles.summary: Researchers in Japan traced a hidden medieval solar storm using ancient tree rings and centuries-old sky observations. The team linked reports of eerie red auroras with spikes of carbon-14 trapped in buried wood, revealing a powerful solar radiation event around 1200 CE. The findings suggest the Sun was far more active at the time, with unusually short solar cycles.
articles.content:
Powerful solar activity can create stunning auroras on Earth, but outside the protection of our planet's magnetic field, the Sun can become extremely dangerous. Violent eruptions such as solar flares and coronal mass ejections can blast high energy particles through space, creating serious risks for astronauts and spacecraft.

Some of these eruptions produce solar proton events (SPEs), during which charged particles race toward Earth at speeds reaching 90% of the speed of light. In 1972, several SPEs erupted between the Apollo 16 and Apollo 17 Moon missions. If astronauts had been exposed during a lunar mission, they could have faced lethal radiation levels. As space agencies prepare for future Moon exploration, scientists are working to better understand these unpredictable solar events.

Researchers at the Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology (OIST) have now developed a new way to uncover evidence of past SPEs. The team combined medieval historical records with ultra precise carbon 14 measurements taken from buried asunaro trees in northern Japan. Using this method, they identified a solar proton event that likely occurred sometime between the winter of 1200 CE and the spring of 1201 CE, a period marked by unusually intense solar activity. The findings were published in the Proceedings of the Japan Academy, Series B.

Professor Hiroko Miyahara from the OIST Solar-Terrestrial Environment and Climate Unit explained: "Previous studies on historical SPEs have focused on rare, extremely powerful events. Our paper provides a basis for detecting sub-extreme SPEs -- events that occur more frequently and are around 10-30% of the size of the most extreme cases, but still hazardous. Sub-extreme SPEs are more challenging to detect, but our method now allows us to efficiently identify them and better understand the conditions under which they are more likely to occur."

Ancient Trees Preserve Clues About Solar Storms

Earth's magnetic field blocks most high energy particles released during SPEs. Near the poles, however, magnetic field lines open into space, allowing some particles to enter the atmosphere. During especially powerful events, these particles collide with atmospheric gases and create carbon 14 compounds that spread around the globe and become trapped inside living organisms.

By analyzing carbon 14 levels in preserved organic material such as ancient buried trees, scientists can track changes in solar activity stretching back thousands of years. The OIST team used an ultra precise measurement technique they spent more than a decade refining. This method can detect much smaller carbon 14 fluctuations than conventional techniques, making it possible to identify weaker "sub-extreme" solar proton events that were previously invisible.

Because the carbon 14 analysis is extremely time intensive, the researchers first needed clues about when unusual solar activity may have occurred.

Medieval Japanese Diary Revealed "Red Lights" in the Sky

One of the key clues came from Meigetsuki, the diary of the Japanese poet and courtier Fujiwara no Teika (1162-1241). In February 1204 CE, he described seeing "red lights in the northern sky over Kyoto."

Solar proton events do not directly create auroras, but they are often linked with the same kinds of solar disturbances that do. That historical observation gave researchers a timeframe to investigate more closely.

The scientists then measured carbon 14 levels in buried asunaro wood recovered from Aomori Prefecture in northern Japan. They discovered spikes in carbon 14 that pointed to a sub-extreme solar proton event. By combining those measurements with dendroclimatic studies -- that is, a dating method based on comparing patterns of tree-ring growth associated with regional climate -- the researchers determined that the event likely occurred sometime between the winter of 1200 CE and the spring of 1201 CE. Historical records from China also described a red aurora visible at unusually low latitudes during that same period.

Evidence of an Exceptionally Active Sun

"The high-precision data not only allowed us to accurately date sub-extreme solar proton events, but it also lets us clearly reconstruct the solar cycles of the period," said Miyahara. "Today, the Sun's activity fluctuates over eleven-year-long cycles, but we've found that the cycle was just seven to eight years long back then, indicating a very active Sun. The SPE we have dated occurred at the peak of one of these cycles."

The research helps fill important gaps in the history of solar activity and improves scientists' understanding of dangerous space weather events. According to Miyahara, carbon 14 analysis alone is not enough. Historical records and other scientific methods are also essential for reconstructing past solar behavior.

"Historical literature provides a candidate time window, and dendroclimatology enables direct intercomparison between detected SPE and reports of sunspots and auroras recorded in literature. Integrated approaches like these are necessary to accurately reconstruct past solar activity, helping us better understand the characteristics of extreme space weather," concluded Miyahara. "For example, while the SPE we found occurred near the peak of the solar cycle, some of the prolonged low-latitude aurora recorded in the literature seems to fall near the minimum of our reconstructed solar cycle. This is unexpected, and we're excited to look further into what solar conditions could cause this."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding historical solar activity by combining ancient tree ring data and medieval records. This discovery of a powerful solar storm from 800 years ago has broad implications for space weather prediction, which is crucial for protecting astronauts, spacecraft, and modern technology. The research provides meaningful insights into solar cycles and space weather hazards, benefiting society's preparedness for future solar events.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover 800-year-old solar storm using ancient trees and medieval records
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Powerful solar storms send high-energy particles toward Earth, which can be dangerous for astronauts and technology. Understanding these storms helps prepare for future space missions and protect our technology.

**What happened** – Researchers in Japan studied ancient tree rings and old Japanese and Chinese writings to find evidence of a strong solar storm around the year 1200 CE. They measured carbon-14 levels in buried trees, which rise when solar particles hit the atmosphere. They also found records describing red lights in the sky, helping date the event.

**Impact** – This discovery shows the Sun was more active 800 years ago, with shorter solar cycles than today. It also reveals smaller but still harmful solar storms called sub-extreme solar proton events, which are harder to detect. Knowing about these helps scientists better predict space weather risks.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to study more ancient trees and historical records to learn about other past solar storms. This will improve understanding of how often these storms happen and what causes them, helping protect astronauts and technology in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Combining ancient tree data and medieval writings, scientists found evidence of a strong solar storm 800 years ago, improving knowledge of the Sun’s activity and space weather risks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une tempête solaire vieille de 800 ans grâce aux arbres anciens et aux archives médiévales
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les puissantes tempêtes solaires envoient des particules énergétiques vers la Terre, ce qui peut être dangereux pour les astronautes et la technologie. Comprendre ces tempêtes aide à mieux se préparer pour les futures missions spatiales et à protéger nos équipements.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs japonais ont étudié les cernes d’arbres anciens et de vieux écrits japonais et chinois pour trouver des preuves d’une forte tempête solaire autour de l’an 1200. Ils ont mesuré le carbone 14 dans des arbres enterrés, qui augmente lorsque les particules solaires frappent l’atmosphère. Ils ont aussi trouvé des récits décrivant des lumières rouges dans le ciel, ce qui a aidé à dater l’événement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le Soleil était plus actif il y a 800 ans, avec des cycles solaires plus courts qu’aujourd’hui. Elle révèle aussi des tempêtes solaires plus petites mais dangereuses, appelées événements solaires protoniques sub-extrêmes, difficiles à détecter. Cela aide les scientifiques à mieux prévoir les risques liés au climat spatial.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’étudier plus d’arbres anciens et de documents historiques pour en apprendre davantage sur d’autres tempêtes solaires passées. Cela améliorera la compréhension de leur fréquence et de leurs causes, aidant à protéger astronautes et technologies à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En combinant les données d’arbres anciens et les écrits médiévaux, les scientifiques ont trouvé la trace d’une forte tempête solaire vieille de 800 ans, améliorant la connaissance de l’activité solaire et des risques liés au climat spatial.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren tormenta solar de hace 800 años usando árboles antiguos y registros medievales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las poderosas tormentas solares envían partículas energéticas hacia la Tierra, lo que puede ser peligroso para astronautas y tecnología. Entender estas tormentas ayuda a prepararse mejor para futuras misiones espaciales y proteger nuestros dispositivos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Japón estudiaron los anillos de árboles antiguos y escritos japoneses y chinos para encontrar evidencia de una fuerte tormenta solar alrededor del año 1200. Midieron niveles de carbono-14 en árboles enterrados, que aumentan cuando partículas solares llegan a la atmósfera. También encontraron registros que describen luces rojas en el cielo, ayudando a fechar el evento.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que el Sol era más activo hace 800 años, con ciclos solares más cortos que ahora. También revela tormentas solares más pequeñas pero peligrosas llamadas eventos solares protones sub-extremos, difíciles de detectar. Esto ayuda a los científicos a predecir mejor los riesgos del clima espacial.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar más árboles antiguos y registros históricos para aprender sobre otras tormentas solares pasadas. Esto mejorará la comprensión sobre la frecuencia y causas de estas tormentas, ayudando a proteger a astronautas y tecnología en el futuro.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Combinando datos de árboles antiguos y escritos medievales, los científicos encontraron evidencia de una fuerte tormenta solar de hace 800 años, mejorando el conocimiento sobre la actividad del Sol y los riesgos del clima espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, stylized winter landscape in northern Japan featuring ancient buried asunaro trees with glowing tree rings subtly illuminated by a warm red aurora stretching across a starry night sky, symbolizing an intense medieval solar storm and its cosmic energy reaching Earth.
